request.question: Who was the professor from the private Ph.D.-granting research university in Chicago that founded a website that is a multimedia archive of the United States Supreme court?

title: V.L. v. E.L.
Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr.
Supreme Court Police
Supreme Court of Belarus
Instituto Tecnológico Autónomo de México
Marshal of the United States Supreme Court
Oyez Project
Supreme Court of the Virgin Islands
United States v. Hamilton
Illinois Institute of Technology
sentences: V.L. v. E.L., 577 U.S. ___ (2016) , is a case decided by the Supreme Court of the United States concerning the adoption rights of same-sex couples.
In 2007, a Georgia Superior Court granted adoption rights to V.L., the partner of E.L., the woman who gave birth to their three children.
However, after moving back to Alabama, the couple split up.
E.L. tried to block V.L. from seeing the children, but V.L. filed a lawsuit seeking visitation and other parental rights.
On September 18, 2015, the Supreme Court of Alabama ruled that the state did not have to recognize the adoption judgment, saying that the Georgia court misapplied its own state law.
The court voided the recognition of the adoption judgment in Alabama.
V.L. petitioned the United States Supreme Court to stay the ruling during her appeal and allow her to see her children.

On December 14, 2015, the Supreme Court stayed the ruling pending their action on a petition for a writ of certiorari filed by V.L. On March 7, 2016, the Supreme Court of the United States reversed the decision of the Alabama Supreme Court by per curiam summary disposition.

Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr. ( ; March 8, 1841 – March 6, 1935) was an American jurist who served as an Associate Justice of the Supreme Court of the United States from 1902 to 1932, and as Acting Chief Justice of the United States from January–February 1930.

Noted for his long service, his concise and pithy opinions and his deference to the decisions of elected legislatures, he is one of the most widely cited United States Supreme Court justices in history, particularly for his "clear and present danger" opinion for a unanimous Court in the 1919 case of "Schenck v. United States", and is one of the most influential American common law judges, honored during his lifetime in Great Britain as well as the United States.
Holmes retired from the Court at the age of 90 years, making him the oldest Justice in the Supreme Court's history.
He also served as an Associate Justice and as Chief Justice on the Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court, and was Weld Professor of Law at the Harvard Law School, of which he was an alumnus.

The Supreme Court of the United States Police is a small U.S. federal law enforcement agency headquartered in the District of Columbia, whose mission is to ensure the integrity of the constitutional mission of the U.S. Supreme Court by protecting the Supreme Court building, the Justices, employees, guests, and visitors.
In accordance with , the Supreme Court Police falls under the jurisdiction of the Marshal of the United States Supreme Court who is appointed by the Supreme Court.
The Marshal and the Supreme Court Police are authorized by to police the Supreme Court Building and protect the Justices, employees of the Court, and visitors to the Court.
The Supreme Court of the Republic of Belarus is the highest-tier court inside of Belarus and acts as the final "court of review."
Its general tasks include the oversight of lower-tier courts and can render justice in areas of general civil and criminal law.
Unlike the United States Supreme Court, constitutional issues are not sent to the Belarusian Supreme Court, but are sent to a separate court called the Constitutional Court.
The judges to the Supreme Court are appointed by the president.
On 1 January 2014 the Supreme Economic Court of Belarus merged with the Supreme Court.
The Instituto Tecnológico Autónomo de México (Mexico Autonomous Institute of Technology), commonly known as ITAM, is a private Ph.D.-granting research university located in Mexico City.

It is one of the nation's most important institutions of higher learning; the best undergraduate Accounting, Business, Economics, International Relations, Law, and Political Science school in Mexico City; and the best business school in Latin America.
Also, it is considered one of Mexico's think tanks and has the highest rank of admission to the Mexican Foreign Service.
The Marshal of the United States Supreme Court is the person, similar to a bailiff, who attends the Supreme Court of the United States.

The Marshal of the United States Supreme Court controls the United States Supreme Court Police, a security police service answerable to the court itself rather than to the president or attorney general.
It handles security for the Supreme Court building and for the justices personally.

The Oyez Project at the Illinois Institute of Technology's Chicago-Kent College of Law is an unofficial online multimedia archive of the Supreme Court of the United States, especially audio of oral arguments.
The website "aims to be a complete and authoritative source for all audio recorded in the Court since <nowiki>[...]</nowiki> October 1955."
The website also includes biographical information of both incumbent and historical justices of the Court and advocates who have argued before the court.
The website was founded by Jerry Goldman, a research professor of law at the Chicago-Kent College of Law at Illinois Institute of Technology.
The Supreme Court of the Virgin Islands is the highest court in the territory of the United States Virgin Islands.
The Supreme Court assumed jurisdiction over all appeals from the Superior Court of the Virgin Islands, a trial level court, on January 29, 2007.
There are three Supreme Court justices who are each appointed by the Governor and confirmed by the Legislature for a ten-year term.
Appointments are for life.
The Supreme Court consists of a Chief Justice and two Associate Justices.
There is no intermediate court of appeals, and the Supreme Court does not have discretion in hearing appeals.

Appeals of Supreme Court decisions were heard by writ of certiorari by the United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit until December 29, 2012, but since then they have been heard by the United States Supreme Court.

United States v. Hamilton, 3 U.S. 17 (1795) , was a United States Supreme Court case in which a defendant committed on a charge of treason was released on bail, despite having been imprisoned upon a warrant of committal by a district court judge.

The Judiciary Act of 1789 stated that "upon all arrests in criminal cases, bail shall be admitted, except where the punishment may be death, in which cases it shall not be admitted but by the supreme or a circuit court, or by a justice of the supreme court, or a judge of a district court, who shall exercise their discretion therein, regarding the nature and circumstances of the offence, and of the evidence, and the usages of law."
Ordinarily, habeas corpus was used to release prisoners held by the judgment of the executive, but not for those who commitment had been authorized by a court order.

Hamilton's attorney argued that the district court judge did not hold a hearing before issuing a warrant for his commitment to jail and that the affidavits alleging treasonous activity were weak, while the government urged that the Judiciary Act did not give the Supreme Court the jurisdiction to review the district court's decision unless there was new information or misconduct.
The Supreme Court set bail, but without addressing either attorney's arguments.

Illinois Institute of Technology, commonly called Illinois Tech or IIT, is a private Ph.D.-granting research university located in Chicago, in the U.S. state of Illinois, with programs in engineering, science, psychology, architecture, business, communications, industrial technology, information technology, design and law.
It traces its history to several 19th century engineering and professional education institutions in the United States.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here. Emit exactly one field named `summary`.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A one-line header naming the primary topic(s) identified in the passages.
- 1–4 short lines enumerating concrete facts directly supported by the passages. Extract entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Do not add or infer facts not present in the passages.
- An explicit statement that names the missing bridge fact or relation needed for the next hop (express in abstract role terms, e.g., "missing bridge fact: [relation] between [candidate entity] and [target attribute]"). If passages supply candidate bridge entities but do not confirm the required relation, label them as "possible bridge entity" and state what is unverified for each.
- 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (highest priority first). Each directive must be concrete and section-aware: specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Early life", "History"), or tight search queries/keywords. Prefer section-level targets and tight searches over broad suggestions.

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` compact: one-line header plus a total of 1–4 brief lines (sentences or bullet lines) covering the factual spans, the missing bridge fact, and the prioritized retrieval directives.
- Preserve exact factual spans from the passages; when quoting, keep quotes very short (<= ~15 words) and only if verbatim in the passages.
- Do not assert a final answer or resolve facts here. If the passages do fully and unambiguously contain the required relation, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but still do not produce a final answer.
- If multiple candidate entities or variant names appear, enumerate the top 1–2 candidates and mark the name as ambiguous, stating what is unverified for each.
- Do not invent facts, hypothesize, or add extraneous context not grounded in the provided passages.

Output expectation: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary as specified above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Priority rules for ambiguity and disambiguation:
- If summary_1 lists multiple candidate anchors or otherwise signals ambiguity, emit a single connector-style query that concatenates the prioritized anchor tokens (use the order or priority visible in summary_1) and then append exactly one short attribute token or a visible year token for disambiguation.
- If summary_1 contains a comma-separated list of individuals and the missing evidence concerns a member, prefer the earliest-listed individual token paired with the visible group token and append the single attribute token member.
- If the question contains a visible year token that disambiguates a title, include that year token before the attribute token in the query.
- If none of the above applies, emit a single-anchor query using the most relevant visible token and at most one short attribute token when the question implies an attribute.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (prefer the form used in the context when there is a name-variant conflict) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Follow these additional concise operational rules when integrating evidence:
- Prefer the context-preferred name form for the preserved first (answer) sentence; when choosing a canonical name for supporting sentences, prefer the context form if there is a name-variant conflict across inputs.
- If a passage explicitly contradicts a preserved context sentence, replace the preserved sentence with a passage-supported first sentence or note the contradiction concisely in that first sentence.
- For each supporting sentence, start with the chosen canonical name and include only attributes explicitly present in the provided passages; if the passages do not assert a requested status or attribute, state that absence briefly rather than inferring the attribute.
- Do not add fields, requests for more retrieval, or change the input/output interface. Keep the `summary` to 1–3 sentences exactly and avoid extra commentary or formatting.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add any new facts or external information.
- First run a mandatory binary detection: determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to extraction.

Conservative extractive tie-breaking (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- When a contiguous longer verbatim phrase in either summary strictly contains a shorter candidate (adds adjacent words that are part of the name or label), emit the longest contiguous verbatim phrase exactly as it appears in the summaries.
- For personal names where one verbatim form is a substring of another, prefer the longest verbatim personal-name span found in either summary; if multiple distinct spans tie for length, prefer the form from `summary_2`.
- If either summary presents an alternate token parenthetically immediately adjacent to a name/place (e.g., canonical short form in parentheses), emit that parenthetical token exactly as shown. If both summaries present parenthetical tokens, prefer the one in `summary_2`.
- If the summaries do not explicitly state the requested relation but exactly one candidate entity of the relevant role appears verbatim across the summaries and no summary contradicts the attribution, emit that single verbatim entity. If multiple distinct candidates appear or there is any contradiction, do not apply this rule and return the unresolved outcome.
- Prefer wording from `summary_2` only as a tie-breaker, except when the longest-contiguous-span or parenthetical-token rules require choosing a longer or parenthetical form present in `summary_1`.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest contiguous verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind"). If multiple adjacent phrases appear, prefer the longest contiguous phrase unless that creates contradiction.
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

Use only the provided summaries as evidence and follow the conservative tie-breaking rules above when extracting the final minimal verbatim span.

response.hop1_query: Who was the professor from the private Ph.D.-granting research university in Chicago that founded a website that is a multimedia archive of the United States Supreme court?
hop1_docs: Oyez Project | The Oyez Project at the Illinois Institute of Technology's Chicago-Kent College of Law is an unofficial online multimedia archive of the Supreme Court of the United States, especially audio of oral arguments.  The website "aims to be a complete and authoritative source for all audio recorded in the Court since <nowiki>[...]</nowiki> October 1955."  The website also includes biographical information of both incumbent and historical justices of the Court and advocates who have argued before the court.  The website was founded by Jerry Goldman, a research professor of law at the Chicago-Kent College of Law at Illinois Institute of Technology.

Geoffrey Sauer | Geoffrey Sauer (born 1968 in Bloomington, Indiana) is an American new media theorist who researches technologies including open source software and collaborative multimedia development in the context of the history of publishing.  He is the director of the open-access electronic text archive the EServer , an electronic text archive, which is according to Alexa the most popular website in the arts and humanities.  He is also the director of the Studio for New Media at Iowa State University, as well as an Assistant Professor of Rhetoric and Professional Communication in the ISU English Department.

Illinois Institute of Technology | Illinois Institute of Technology, commonly called Illinois Tech or IIT, is a private Ph.D.-granting research university located in Chicago, in the U.S. state of Illinois, with programs in engineering, science, psychology, architecture, business, communications, industrial technology, information technology, design and law.  It traces its history to several 19th century engineering and professional education institutions in the United States.

Xiaoliang Sunney Xie | Professor Xiaoliang Sunney Xie (; born 1962 in Beijing, China) is considered a founding father of single-molecule biophysical chemistry and single-molecule enzymology.  He received his B.Sc.  in Chemistry from Peking University in 1984, and his Ph.D. in Physical Chemistry in 1990 from University of California at San Diego.  After a brief postdoctoral appointment at University of Chicago, he joined Pacific Northwest National Laboratory, where he rose from Senior Research Scientist to Chief Scientist.  In 1998, he became the first tenured professor at Harvard University among Chinese Scholars who came to the United States since the Reform in China.

Willamette University College of Law | Willamette University College of Law is a private law school located in Salem, Oregon, United States.  Founded in 1842, Willamette University is the oldest university in the Western United States.  The College of Law, which was founded in 1883 and is the oldest law school in the Pacific Northwest, has approximately 50 law professors (full-time and adjunct) and enrolls about 300 students, with about 100 of those of students enrolled in their first year of law school.  The campus is located across the street from the Oregon State Capitol and the Oregon Supreme Court Building.

Ulysses Sherman Grant | Ulysses Sherman Grant (February 14, 1867 in Moline, Illinois - September 21, 1932 in Chicago, Illinois) was an American geologist.  He was the son of Lewis A. Grant.  He graduated from the University of Minnesota with B.S. in 1888 and received a Ph.D. from Johns Hopkins University in 1893.  In 1899, he became professor of geology at Northwestern University.

SCOTUSblog | SCOTUSblog is a law blog written by lawyers, law professors, and law students about the Supreme Court of the United States (sometimes abbreviated "SCOTUS").  The blog was formerly sponsored by Bloomberg Law.  The site tracks cases before the Court from the certiorari stage through the merits stage.  The site live blogs as the Court announces opinions and grants cases, and sometimes has information on the Court's actions published before either the Court or any other news source does.  The site frequently hosts symposiums with leading experts on the cases before the Court.  The site comprehensively covers all of the cases argued before the Court and maintains an archive of the briefing and other documents in each case.
response.summary_1:
Oyez Project founder / Illinois Institute of Technology

- Jerry Goldman — founder of the Oyez Project; described as a "research professor of law" at Chicago‑Kent College of Law, Illinois Institute of Technology.
- Illinois Institute of Technology — described as a "private Ph.D.-granting research university" located in Chicago.

response.hop2_query: Jerry Goldman Illinois Institute of Technology professor
hop2_docs: Oyez Project | The Oyez Project at the Illinois Institute of Technology's Chicago-Kent College of Law is an unofficial online multimedia archive of the Supreme Court of the United States, especially audio of oral arguments.  The website "aims to be a complete and authoritative source for all audio recorded in the Court since <nowiki>[...]</nowiki> October 1955."  The website also includes biographical information of both incumbent and historical justices of the Court and advocates who have argued before the court.  The website was founded by Jerry Goldman, a research professor of law at the Chicago-Kent College of Law at Illinois Institute of Technology.

Ron Goldman (mathematician) | Ronald Neil Goldman is a Professor of Computer Science at Rice University in Houston, Texas.  Professor Goldman received his B.S. in Mathematics from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in 1968 and his M.A. and Ph.D. in Mathematics from Johns Hopkins University in 1973.

Jeffrey S. Moore | Jeffrey Scott Moore (born 1962, Joliet, Illinois) is the Murchison-Mallory Professor of Chemistry and a Professor of Materials Science & Engineering at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign.  He has received awards for both teaching and research, and as of 2014, was named a Howard Hughes Medical Institute Professor.  In 2017, he was named Director of the Beckman Institute for Advanced Science and Technology at the University of Illinois, after serving as Interim Director for one year. .
List of Illinois Institute of Technology alumni | This list of Illinois Institute of Technology alumni includes graduates and non-graduate former students of Illinois Institute of Technology.

Jerry A. Hausman | Jerry Allen Hausman (born May 5, 1946) is the John and Jennie S. MacDonald Professor of Economics at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology and a notable econometrician.  He has published numerous influential papers in microeconometrics.  Hausman is the recipient of several prestigious awards including the John Bates Clark Medal in 1985 and the Frisch Medal in 1980.

Lori Andrews | Lori B. Andrews is an American professor of law.  She is on the faculty of Illinois Institute of Technology Chicago-Kent College of Law and serves as Director of IIT's Institute for Science, Law, and Technology.  In 2002, she was a visiting professor at Princeton University.  She received her B.A. "summa cum laude" from Yale College and her J.D. from Yale Law School.  Andrews is a Fellow of the Hastings Center.

No innovation without representation | No innovation without representation is a democratic ideal of ensuring that everyone involved gets a chance to be represented fairly in technological developments.  Langdon Winner (Thomas Phelan Chair of Humanities and Social Sciences in the Department of Science and Technology Studies at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute of Troy, NY) states that groups and social interests likely to be affected by a particular kind of technological change ought to be represented at an early stage in defining exactly what that technology will be.  It is the idea that relevant parties have a say in technological developments and are not left in the dark.  It has been spoken about by current professor Massimiano Bucchi (Ph.D.  Social and Political science, European University Institute, 1997) of the University of Trento in Italy.  This ideal does not require the public to become experts on the topics of science and engineering, it only asks that the opinions and ideas be heard before making drastic decisions, as talked about by Steven L. Goldman (Andrew W. Mellon Distinguished Professor in Humanities at Lehigh University).

response.summary_2: Jerry Goldman — founder of the Oyez Project; described as a "research professor of law" at Chicago‑Kent College of Law, Illinois Institute of Technology. The Oyez Project is an unofficial online multimedia archive of the Supreme Court of the United States, especially focused on audio of oral arguments.
